name: Splunk HTTP Event Collector (HEC)
what_it_does: Splunk's HTTP endpoint for ingesting events, logs, and metrics directly from applications without a forwarder agent.
use_cases: Stream agent logs and events to Splunk for security monitoring and alerting without a Splunk forwarder
Send structured JSON events from AI pipelines for search and analysis in Splunk dashboards
Ingest metrics with timestamps for time-series analysis in Splunk's metrics index
Forward application errors to Splunk SIEM for correlation with security events
Batch send historical events with custom timestamps for log replay and analysis
not_for: Teams without Splunk Enterprise or Splunk Cloud — HEC requires a running Splunk instance
Low-latency applications where 100ms+ HEC ingestion latency is unacceptable
Raw log file shipping — use Splunk Universal Forwarder for file-based ingestion
best_when: Best for applications already running in Splunk environments that need direct API-based event ingestion.
avoid_when: Avoid when you don't have Splunk — use Elasticsearch/Loki or managed logging for new setups.

auth.notes: HEC token passed as Authorization: Splunk <token> header. Tokens are configured per input in Splunk.

pricing.paid_tiers: Splunk Enterprise (license-based on GB/day) or Splunk Cloud (subscription)
pricing.notes: HEC is a feature of Splunk — requires Splunk Enterprise or Cloud license. Splunk Cloud Dev licenses available for development.

performance.rate_limits: Default 1MB per request; configurable max content length; throughput depends on Splunk indexer capacity

agent_readiness.error_message_notes: HEC returns JSON with text and code fields — HTTP 400 with code 7 means invalid data format.
agent_readiness.idempotency_notes: HEC does not deduplicate events — duplicate sends create duplicate index entries.
known_agent_gotchas: HEC token must have 'Enable indexer acknowledgment' turned on to use the ack endpoint for guaranteed delivery — off by default
Batch events in a single request by sending multiple JSON objects as a newline-delimited stream, not an array
Event time field uses Unix epoch seconds (not milliseconds) — wrong timestamp format causes events to index with wrong time
HEC returns HTTP 200 even when the indexer queue is full if acknowledgment is disabled — monitor Splunk indexer queues separately
SSL certificate validation must be configured — self-signed certs on Splunk require adding CA cert or disabling verify_ssl in client

security_score_components.security_notes: HEC tokens should be rotated regularly and scoped to specific source types and indexes. TLS required in production.
